Student Experiences in Academic Environments

  • Show All Code

  • View Source

PSY 120L Feedback Study Report

Author

Riley Elzey

Published

May 26, 2026

About This Report

This reproducible report documents the data preparation, variable construction, descriptive statistics, primary analyses, and figures for this study.

The report is intended to accompany a manuscript-style research paper. It is not the full paper itself. Instead, it shows how the reported analyses were produced from the dataset using a transparent and reproducible Quarto workflow.

Study Overview

This study examined whether the type of academic feedback students receive affects their self-efficacy and achievement motivation, and whether perceived parental warmth moderates these effects. Participants were randomly assigned to read either an autonomy-supportive or controlling feedback scenario from a professor.

Self-efficacy was measured using the General Self-Efficacy Scale (GSE) and achievement motivation was measured using the Rays Academic Motivation Scale (RAMS). Perceived parental warmth was assessed using the Parenting Assessment Questionnaire (PAQ) and median-split into high and low groups.

A 2 × 2 between-subjects factorial ANOVA was used to test the main effects and interaction.

Setup and Software Environment

The following packages were loaded to support data wrangling, scale construction, statistical modeling, and visualization throughout this report.

Load Packages

Show code 
library(tidyverse)  # data manipulation + plotting
library(psych)      # scale construction + reliability
library(afex)       # ANOVA models + effect sizes
library(emmeans)    # estimated marginal means + confidence intervals

Data Source and Analysis Dataset

The data for this study were collected via Qualtrics and exported as a CSV file. The raw dataset was imported into R for all subsequent cleaning and analysis.

Import Dataset

Show code 
study_raw <- read_csv("data/Autonomy_clean.csv")

Check Imported Dataset

Show code 
dim(study_raw)
[1] 79 65
Show code 
names(study_raw)
 [1] "ResponseId"          "DistributionChannel" "Consent"

[10] "GSE_5"               "GSE_6"               "GSE_7"

[19] "RAMS_4"              "RAMS_5"              "RAMS_6"             
[22] "RAMS_7"              "RAMS_8"              "RAMS_9"             
[25] "RAMS_10"             "RAMS_11"             "RAMS_12"

Show code 
head(study_raw)

The raw dataset contains 79 rows previewed above across 65 total columns, including participant identifiers, condition assignment, consent status, and all scale items for the GSE, RAMS, and PAQ measures. Each row represents one participant’s survey responses.

Create Analysis Dataset

Show code 
study_vars <- c(
  
  "condition", "age", "gender", "year",
  
  "GSE_1", "GSE_2", "GSE_3", "GSE_4", "GSE_5", "GSE_6",
  "GSE_7", "GSE_8", "GSE_9", "GSE_10",

  "RAMS_1", "RAMS_2", "RAMS_3", "RAMS_4", "RAMS_5", "RAMS_6",
  "RAMS_7", "RAMS_8", "RAMS_9", "RAMS_10", "RAMS_11", "RAMS_12",
  "RAMS_13", "RAMS_14",

  "PAQ1_1", "PAQ1_2", "PAQ1_3", "PAQ1_4", "PAQ1_5", "PAQ1_6",
  "PAQ1_7", "PAQ1_8", "PAQ1_9", "PAQ1_10", "PAQ1_11", "PAQ1_12", 
  "PAQ1_13", "PAQ1_14",  "PAQ1_15",
  
  "PAQ2_1", "PAQ2_2", "PAQ2_3", "PAQ2_4", "PAQ2_5", "PAQ2_6", 
  "PAQ2_7", "PAQ2_8", "PAQ2_9", "PAQ2_10", "PAQ2_11", "PAQ2_12",
  "PAQ2_13", "PAQ2_14", "PAQ2_15"
)


rams_items_raw <- paste0("RAMS_", 1:14)
paq_items <- c(paste0("PAQ1_", 1:15), paste0("PAQ2_", 1:15))

study_data <- study_raw %>%
  select(all_of(study_vars)) %>%
  mutate(across(-condition, as.numeric)) %>%
  filter(if_all(all_of(paq_items), ~ !is.na(.))) %>%
  filter(if_all(all_of(rams_items_raw), ~ !is.na(.)))

Check Analysis Dataset

Show code 
length(study_vars)
[1] 58
Show code

[1] 76 58

[13] "GSE_9"     "GSE_10"    "RAMS_1"    "RAMS_2"    "RAMS_3"    "RAMS_4"

[31] "PAQ1_3"    "PAQ1_4"    "PAQ1_5"    "PAQ1_6"    "PAQ1_7"    "PAQ1_8"   
[37] "PAQ1_9"    "PAQ1_10"   "PAQ1_11"   "PAQ1_12"   "PAQ1_13"   "PAQ1_14"  
[43] "PAQ1_15"   "PAQ2_1"    "PAQ2_2"    "PAQ2_3"    "PAQ2_4"    "PAQ2_5"   
[49] "PAQ2_6"    "PAQ2_7"    "PAQ2_8"    "PAQ2_9"    "PAQ2_10"   "PAQ2_11"  
[55] "PAQ2_12"   "PAQ2_13"   "PAQ2_14"   "PAQ2_15"
Show code 
study_data %>% count(condition)

The analysis dataset contains 76 participants across 58 variables. The variable list confirms that all scale items are present, including 10 GSE items, 14 RAMS items, 30 PAQ items, and the experimental condition variable. The dataset is ready for scale scoring and analysis.

Variable Preparation

This section prepares all variables needed for analysis. Scales scores were computed for self-efficacy (GSE), achievement motivation (RAM), and parenting warmth (PAQ) by summing each participant’s item responses. Perceived parental warmth was median-split into high and low groups to create the moderator variable used in the ANOVA. A unique participant ID was assigned to each row.

Prepare Participant ID and Experimental Condition

Show code 
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    id = row_number(),
    condition = factor(
      condition,
      levels = c(0, 1),
      labels = c("Control", "Autonomy")
    )
  )

A unique numeric ID was assigned to each participant and the experimental condition variable was converted to a labeled factor with two levels (Control and Autonomy).

Check Experimental Condition Coding

Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(condition) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Condition", "n")
  )
Condition n
Control 38
Autonomy 38

The “Control” condition contained 38 participants and the “Autonomy” condition contained 38 participants, for a total of 76 participants included in the analysis.

Check Prepared Analysis Dataset

Show code 
dim(study_data)
[1] 76 59
Show code 
names(study_data)
 [1] "condition" "age"       "gender"    "year"      "GSE_1"     "GSE_2"    
 [7] "GSE_3"     "GSE_4"     "GSE_5"     "GSE_6"     "GSE_7"     "GSE_8"    
[13] "GSE_9"     "GSE_10"    "RAMS_1"    "RAMS_2"    "RAMS_3"    "RAMS_4"   
[19] "RAMS_5"    "RAMS_6"    "RAMS_7"    "RAMS_8"    "RAMS_9"    "RAMS_10"  
[25] "RAMS_11"   "RAMS_12"   "RAMS_13"   "RAMS_14"   "PAQ1_1"    "PAQ1_2"   
[31] "PAQ1_3"    "PAQ1_4"    "PAQ1_5"    "PAQ1_6"    "PAQ1_7"    "PAQ1_8"   
[37] "PAQ1_9"    "PAQ1_10"   "PAQ1_11"   "PAQ1_12"   "PAQ1_13"   "PAQ1_14"  
[43] "PAQ1_15"   "PAQ2_1"    "PAQ2_2"    "PAQ2_3"    "PAQ2_4"    "PAQ2_5"   
[49] "PAQ2_6"    "PAQ2_7"    "PAQ2_8"    "PAQ2_9"    "PAQ2_10"   "PAQ2_11"  
[55] "PAQ2_12"   "PAQ2_13"   "PAQ2_14"   "PAQ2_15"   "id"

The final analysis dataset contains 76 participants across 59 variables, confirming that all scale items, demographic variables, condition assignment, and the participant ID are present and ready for analysis.

Reverse-Code Scale Items

Items requiring reverse coding were transformed before scale scores were computed.

Show code 
# Reverse-code selected RAM items
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    RAMS_1r = 4 - RAMS_1, # reverse-codes RAMS_1
    RAMS_2r = 4 - RAMS_2, # reverse-codes RAMS_2
    RAMS_7r = 4 - RAMS_7, # reverse-codes RAMS_7
    RAMS_11r = 4 - RAMS_11, #reverse-codes RAMS_11
    RAMS_12r = 4 - RAMS_12, #reverse-codes RAMS_12
    RAMS_13r = 4 - RAMS_13, #reverse-codes RAMS_13
    RAMS_14r = 4 - RAMS_14, #reverse-codes RAMS_14
    )
# Compare original and reversed values
study_data %>%
  select(RAMS_2, RAMS_2r) %>% # selects variables for comparison
  head() # displays first rows

Check Reverse Coding

Show code 
study_data %>%
  select(RAMS_1,RAMS_1r) %>%
  head(10) %>%
  knitr::kable()
RAMS_1 RAMS_1r
2 2
2 2
1 3
1 3
1 3
2 2
1 3
1 3
3 1
3 1

The reverse coding was applied correctly to the RAMS_1 item.

Create Scale Scores

The scale scores were created by first defining the items belonging to each scale. The GSE scale includes 10 self-efficacy items, the RAMS scale includes 14 achievement motivation items with some reverse-coded versions, and the PAQ items are grouped by parenting style (Authoritarian, Authoritative, Permissive). Composite scores were then computed by summing each participants responses across the relevant items.

Show code 
# Define GSE scale items
GSE_items <- c("GSE_1","GSE_2","GSE_3","GSE_4", "GSE_5",
               "GSE_6","GSE_7","GSE_8","GSE_9","GSE_10"
               )

# Define RAM scale items
RAM_items <- c( "RAMS_1r","RAMS_2r","RAMS_3","RAMS_4","RAMS_5","RAMS_6","RAMS_7r","RAMS_8","RAMS_9","RAMS_10","RAMS_11r","RAMS_12r","RAMS_13r","RAMS_14r"
               )
# Check that all items exist in dataset
setNames(RAM_items %in% names(study_data), RAM_items) # verifies item presence
 RAMS_1r  RAMS_2r   RAMS_3   RAMS_4   RAMS_5   RAMS_6  RAMS_7r   RAMS_8 
    TRUE     TRUE     TRUE     TRUE     TRUE     TRUE     TRUE     TRUE 
  RAMS_9  RAMS_10 RAMS_11r RAMS_12r RAMS_13r RAMS_14r 
    TRUE     TRUE     TRUE     TRUE     TRUE     TRUE
Show code 
# Define permissive PAQ items 
PerPAQ_items <- c("PAQ1_1", "PAQ1_6", "PAQ1_10", "PAQ1_13", 
                  "PAQ1_14", "PAQ2_2", "PAQ2_4", "PAQ2_6", 
                  "PAQ2_9", "PAQ2_13"
                  )

# Define authoritarian PAQ items 
ATNPAQ_items <- c("PAQ1_2", "PAQ1_3", "PAQ1_7", "PAQ1_9", 
                  "PAQ1_12", "PAQ2_1", "PAQ2_3", "PAQ2_10", 
                  "PAQ2_11", "PAQ2_14"
                  )

# Define authoritative PAQ items 
ATEPAQ_items <-c("PAQ1_4", "PAQ1_5", "PAQ1_8", "PAQ1_11", 
                 "PAQ1_15", "PAQ2_5", "PAQ2_7", "PAQ2_8", 
                 "PAQ2_12", "PAQ2_15"
                 )

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    GSE_scale  = rowSums(across(all_of(GSE_items)),  na.rm = FALSE),
    RAM_scale  = rowSums(across(all_of(RAM_items)),  na.rm = FALSE),
    PerPAQ_scale  = rowSums(across(all_of(PerPAQ_items)),  na.rm = FALSE),
    ATNPAQ_scale  = rowSums(across(all_of(ATNPAQ_items)), na.rm = FALSE),
    ATEPAQ_scale  = rowSums(across(all_of(ATEPAQ_items)), na.rm = FALSE)
  )

Check Scale Scores

Show code 
study_data %>%
  select(GSE_scale, RAM_scale, PerPAQ_scale, ATNPAQ_scale, ATEPAQ_scale) %>%
  summary()
   GSE_scale       RAM_scale      PerPAQ_scale    ATNPAQ_scale  
 Min.   :20.00   Min.   :14.00   Min.   :11.00   Min.   :13.00  
 1st Qu.:29.00   1st Qu.:21.00   1st Qu.:20.75   1st Qu.:24.75  
 Median :31.00   Median :24.00   Median :26.50   Median :32.00  
 Mean   :31.16   Mean   :24.64   Mean   :25.92   Mean   :30.91  
 3rd Qu.:34.00   3rd Qu.:28.25   3rd Qu.:30.00   3rd Qu.:36.00  
 Max.   :40.00   Max.   :37.00   Max.   :41.00   Max.   :47.00  
  ATEPAQ_scale  
 Min.   :14.00  
 1st Qu.:27.00  
 Median :34.00  
 Mean   :33.13  
 3rd Qu.:39.00  
 Max.   :50.00
Show code 
study_data %>%
  summarise(
    GSE_missing = mean(is.na(GSE_scale)),
    RAM_missing = mean(is.na(RAM_scale)),
    PerPAQ_missing = mean(is.na(PerPAQ_scale)),
    ATNPAQ_missing = mean(is.na(ATNPAQ_scale)),
    ATEPAQ_missing = mean(is.na(ATEPAQ_scale))
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
GSE_missing RAM_missing PerPAQ_missing ATNPAQ_missing ATEPAQ_missing
0 0 0 0 0

This scale scores check confirms that all five scale scores were successfully computed with no missing values across any of the scales.

Create Parental Warmth Groups

Perceived parental warmth groups were created using a median split of scales. Participants scoring above the median were classified as “High Warmth” and those at or bellow as “Low Warmth”, creating the moderator variable used in the ANOVA.

Show code 
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(pwarmth = case_when(
    ATNPAQ_scale > ATEPAQ_scale & ATNPAQ_scale > PerPAQ_scale ~"low", TRUE~"high"
  ))

study_data %>%
  count(pwarmth) %>%
  knitr::kable()
pwarmth n
high 48
low 28

The parental warmth grouping resulted in 48 participants reporting having “High Warmth” and 28 reporting having “Low Warmth”.

Check Scale Reliability

Scale reliability was checked using Cronbach’s alpha and McDonald’s omega for each measure. Values at or above .70 are considered acceptable, while values above .80 indicate good reliability.

Show code 
GSE_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(GSE_items))
RAM_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(RAM_items))
PerPAQ_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(PerPAQ_items))
ATNPAQ_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(ATNPAQ_items))
ATEPAQ_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(ATEPAQ_items))

GSE_alpha <- psych::alpha(GSE_item_df)
GSE_omega <- psych::omega(GSE_item_df, plot = FALSE)

RAM_alpha <- psych::alpha(RAM_item_df)
RAM_omega <- psych::omega(RAM_item_df, plot = FALSE)

PerPAQ_alpha <- psych::alpha(PerPAQ_item_df)
PerPAQ_omega <- psych::omega(PerPAQ_item_df, plot = FALSE)

ATNPAQ_alpha <- psych::alpha(ATNPAQ_item_df)
ATNPAQ_omega <- psych::omega(ATNPAQ_item_df, plot = FALSE)

ATEPAQ_alpha <- psych::alpha(ATEPAQ_item_df)
ATEPAQ_omega <- psych::omega(ATEPAQ_item_df, plot = FALSE)

reliability_table <- tibble(
  scale = c(
    "General Self-Efficacy",
    "Achievement Motivation",
    "Permissive Parenting",
    "Authoritarian Parenting",
    "Authoritative Parenting"
  ),
  alpha = c(
    GSE_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    RAM_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    PerPAQ_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    ATNPAQ_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    ATEPAQ_alpha$total$raw_alpha
  ),
  omega = c(
    GSE_omega$omega.tot,
    RAM_omega$omega.tot,
    PerPAQ_omega$omega.tot,
    ATNPAQ_omega$omega.tot,
    ATEPAQ_omega$omega.tot
  )
)

reliability_table %>%
  mutate(
    alpha = round(alpha, 2),
    omega = round(omega, 2)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Scale", "Cronbach's alpha", "McDonald's omega")
  )
Scale Cronbach’s alpha McDonald’s omega
General Self-Efficacy 0.86 0.90
Achievement Motivation 0.74 0.80
Permissive Parenting 0.76 0.83
Authoritarian Parenting 0.88 0.91
Authoritative Parenting 0.87 0.90

The five scales demonstrated acceptable to excellent internal consistency.

Descriptive Statistics

Descriptive statistics were computed for all five scale scores to examine the distribution, central tendency, and variability of responses prior to running inferential analyses.

Scale Score Descriptives

Show code 
study_data %>%
  select(
    GSE_scale,
    RAM_scale,
    PerPAQ_scale,
    ATNPAQ_scale,
    ATEPAQ_scale
  ) %>%
  psych::describe() %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
vars n mean sd median trimmed mad min max range skew kurtosis se
GSE_scale 1 76 31.16 4.41 31.0 31.26 3.71 20 40 20 -0.18 -0.08 0.51
RAM_scale 2 76 24.64 5.47 24.0 24.50 4.45 14 37 23 0.32 -0.75 0.63
PerPAQ_scale 3 76 25.92 6.53 26.5 25.85 6.67 11 41 30 0.05 -0.51 0.75
ATNPAQ_scale 4 76 30.91 8.12 32.0 31.06 5.93 13 47 34 -0.20 -0.57 0.93
ATEPAQ_scale 5 76 33.13 8.17 34.0 33.44 8.15 14 50 36 -0.34 -0.45 0.94

Across all 76 participants, self-efficacy scores averaged 31.16 (SD = 4.41), and achievement motivation scores averaged 24.64 (SD = 5.47). For parenting style, permissive parenting averaged 25.92 (SD = 6.53), authoritarian parenting averaged 30.91 (SD = 8.12), and authoritative parenting averaged 33.13 (SD = 8.17). All scales distributions appeared approximately normal based on the similarity between means and medians.

Correlation Tests (GSE & RAM)

Show code 
# Correlations for the general self-efficacy and achievement motivation scales
psych::corr.test(
  study_data %>% select(GSE_scale, RAM_scale)
)
Call:psych::corr.test(x = study_data %>% select(GSE_scale, RAM_scale))
Correlation matrix 
          GSE_scale RAM_scale
GSE_scale      1.00     -0.21
RAM_scale     -0.21      1.00
Sample Size 
[1] 76
Probability values (Entries above the diagonal are adjusted for multiple tests.) 
          GSE_scale RAM_scale
GSE_scale      0.00      0.06
RAM_scale      0.06      0.00

 To see confidence intervals of the correlations, print with the short=FALSE option
Show code 
# Scatterplots with labeled axes
ggplot(study_data, aes(x = GSE_scale, y = RAM_scale)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    x = "GSE Score", y = "RAM Score"
  )

Primary Analyses

This section includes the analyses of Academic Feedback Type and Perceived Parental Warmth on Self-Efficacy.

General Self-Efficacy Model

A 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA was conducted to examine the effects of the Academic Feedback Condition and Perceived Parental Warmth on self efficacy.

Show code 
# 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA prediction Self-Efficacy scores
# DV: GSE_scale
# Factors: Condition and pwarmth

a1_SE <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "GSE_scale",
  between = c("condition", "pwarmth"),
  
data = study_data
)

a1_SE
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: GSE_scale
             Effect    df   MSE    F   ges p.value
1         condition 1, 72 19.94 0.07 <.001    .791
2           pwarmth 1, 72 19.94 0.01 <.001    .940
3 condition:pwarmth 1, 72 19.94 1.02  .014    .315
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

This suggests there to be no significant main effect of feedback condition on self-efficacy, F(1, 72) = 0.07, p = .791, η² < .001, and no significant main effect of parental warmth, F(1, 72) = 0.01, p = .940, η² < .001, and no significant interaction between condition and parental warmth, F(1, 72) = 1.02, p = .315, η² = .014.

Estimated Means for Self-Efficacy

Show code 
emmeans(a1_SE, ~ condition)
 condition emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Control     31.3 0.751 72     29.8     32.8
 Autonomy    31.0 0.751 72     29.5     32.5

Results are averaged over the levels of: pwarmth 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_SE, ~ pwarmth)
 pwarmth emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 high      31.2 0.645 72     29.9     32.5
 low       31.1 0.844 72     29.4     32.8

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_SE, ~ condition * pwarmth)
 condition pwarmth emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Control   high      30.8 0.911 72     29.0     32.6
 Autonomy  high      31.6 0.911 72     29.8     33.4
 Control   low       31.8 1.190 72     29.4     34.2
 Autonomy  low       30.4 1.190 72     28.0     32.8

Confidence level used: 0.95

Estimated marginal means indicated that self-efficacy scores were similar across both feedback conditions.

Achievement Motivation Model

A 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA was conducted using the same factors to examine the effects of the Academic Feedback Condition and Perceived Parental Warmth on achievement motivation.

Show code 
a1_RAM <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "RAM_scale",
  between = c("condition", "pwarmth"),
  data    = study_data %>%
              filter(!is.na(RAM_scale), !is.na(pwarmth), !is.na(condition)) %>%
              mutate(
                condition = factor(condition),
                pwarmth   = factor(pwarmth)
              )
)

a1_RAM
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: RAM_scale
             Effect    df   MSE      F   ges p.value
1         condition 1, 72 29.92   0.02 <.001    .902
2           pwarmth 1, 72 29.92 2.88 +  .038    .094
3 condition:pwarmth 1, 72 29.92   0.00 <.001    .945
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The ANOVA revealed no significant main effect of feedback condition on achievement motivation, F(1, 72) = 0.02, p = .902, η² < .001, no significant main effect of parental warmth, F(1, 72) = 2.88, p = .094, η² = 0.038, and no significant interaction between condition and parental warmth, F(1, 72) = 0.00, p = .945, η² < .001.

Estimated Means for Achievement Motivation

Show code 
emmeans(a1_RAM, ~ condition)
 condition emmean   SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Control     24.4 0.92 72     22.6     26.3
 Autonomy    24.3 0.92 72     22.4     26.1

Results are averaged over the levels of: pwarmth 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_RAM, ~ pwarmth)
 pwarmth emmean   SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 high      25.5 0.79 72     23.9     27.0
 low       23.2 1.03 72     21.2     25.3

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_RAM, ~ condition * pwarmth)
 condition pwarmth emmean   SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Control   high      25.6 1.12 72     23.4     27.8
 Autonomy  high      25.3 1.12 72     23.1     27.6
 Control   low       23.3 1.46 72     20.4     26.2
 Autonomy  low       23.2 1.46 72     20.3     26.1

Confidence level used: 0.95

Estimated marginal means indicated that achievement motivation scores were similar across both feedback conditions, with the controlling condition (M = 24.4, 95% CI [22.6, 26.3]) and autonomy-supportive condition (M = 24.3, 95% CI [22.4, 26.1]) showing nearly identical means. High warmth participants (M = 25.5, 95% CI [23.9, 27.0]) scored slightly higher than low warmth participants (M = 23.2, 95% CI [21.2, 25.3]), though this difference was not statistically significant. Cell means ranged from 23.2 to 25.6 across all four conditions, consistent with the non-significant interaction.

Figures

The following figures display the estimated marginal means for each dependent variable across the four conditions created by crossing Academic Feedback Condition (Control vs. Autonomy) and Perceived Parental Warmth (High vs. Low). The error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

Self Efficacy Interaction Plot

Show code 
# Re-run ANOVA
a1_SE <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "GSE_scale",
  between = c("condition", "pwarmth"),
  data = study_data
)

# Create cell means
SE_cell_means <- as.data.frame(
  emmeans(a1_SE, ~ condition * pwarmth)
)

p_SE_bar <- ggplot(
  SE_cell_means,
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = pwarmth
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Feedback Condition",
    y = "Mean Self-Efficacy Score",
    fill = "Parental Warmth"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("lightblue", "salmon"))

p_SE_bar

Figure 1. This figure displays the cell means for self-efficacy across feedback condition and perceived parental warmth. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

Achievement Motivation Interaction Plot

Show code 
p_RAM_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a1_RAM, ~ condition * pwarmth)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = pwarmth
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Feedback Condition",
    y = "Mean Achievement Motivation",
    fill = "Parental Warmth"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("lightblue", "salmon"))

p_RAM_bar

Figure 2. This figure displays the cell means for achievement motivation across feedback condition and perceived parental warmth. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

Export Figures

Show code 
if (!dir.exists("figures")) {
  dir.create("figures")
}

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/self_efficacy_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_SE_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/achievement_motivation_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_RAM_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

Both interaction plots were exported as svg files to the figures folder for use in the final poster and report. Figure 1 displays the self-efficacy interaction plot and Figure 2 displays the achievement motivation interaction plot.

Results Summary

Overall, the results of this study did not support our hypotheses. Participants who received autonomy-supportive academic feedback did not report higher self-efficacy or achievement motivation compared to those who received controlling feedback. Similarly, perceived parental warmth did not significantly influence either outcome. The interaction between feedback condition and parental warmth was also non-significant for both self-efficacy and achievement motivation, suggesting that parental warmth did not change how students responded to different types of academic feedback. These null findings may reflect limitations in sample size, the use of a hypothetical feedback scenario, or the possibility that a single exposure to feedback is not enough to meaningfully shift self-efficacy or motivation.

Reproducibility Information

This section documents the R version, operating system, and package versions used to run and render this report. Recording this information makes it possible for others to replicate our analyses using the same software environment.

---
title: "Student Experiences in Academic Environments"
subtitle: "PSY 120L Feedback Study Report"
author: "Riley Elzey"
date: 5/26/2026
format: 
  html:
    theme: sandstone  
    toc: true
    toc-title: "Report Sections"
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: false
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    code-tools: true
    df-print: paged
    highlight-style: tango
    embed-resources: true
    smooth-scroll: true

  warning: false
  message: false

  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

## About This Report

This reproducible report documents the data preparation, variable
construction, descriptive statistics, primary analyses, and figures for
this study.

The report is intended to accompany a manuscript-style research paper.
It is not the full paper itself. Instead, it shows how the reported
analyses were produced from the dataset using a transparent and
reproducible Quarto workflow.

## Study Overview

This study examined whether the type of academic feedback students
receive affects their self-efficacy and achievement motivation, and
whether perceived parental warmth moderates these effects. Participants
were randomly assigned to read either an autonomy-supportive or
controlling feedback scenario from a professor.

Self-efficacy was measured using the General Self-Efficacy Scale (GSE)
and achievement motivation was measured using the Rays Academic
Motivation Scale (RAMS). Perceived parental warmth was assessed using
the Parenting Assessment Questionnaire (PAQ) and median-split into high
and low groups.

A 2 × 2 between-subjects factorial ANOVA was used to test the main
effects and interaction.

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center"
)

options(stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Setup and Software Environment

The following packages were loaded to support data wrangling, scale
construction, statistical modeling, and visualization throughout this
report.

**Load Packages**

```{r}
#| label: load_packages
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)  # data manipulation + plotting
library(psych)      # scale construction + reliability
library(afex)       # ANOVA models + effect sizes
library(emmeans)    # estimated marginal means + confidence intervals
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Data Source and Analysis Dataset

The data for this study were collected via Qualtrics and exported as a
CSV file. The raw dataset was imported into R for all subsequent
cleaning and analysis.

### Import Dataset

```{r}
#| label: import_data
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_raw <- read_csv("data/Autonomy_clean.csv")
```

### Check Imported Dataset

```{r}
#| label: check_imported_data
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

dim(study_raw)

names(study_raw)

head(study_raw)
```

The raw dataset contains 79 rows previewed above across 65 total
columns, including participant identifiers, condition assignment,
consent status, and all scale items for the GSE, RAMS, and PAQ measures.
Each row represents one participant's survey responses.

### Create Analysis Dataset

```{r}
#| label: create_analysis_dataset
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_vars <- c(
  
  "condition", "age", "gender", "year",
  
  "GSE_1", "GSE_2", "GSE_3", "GSE_4", "GSE_5", "GSE_6",
  "GSE_7", "GSE_8", "GSE_9", "GSE_10",

  "RAMS_1", "RAMS_2", "RAMS_3", "RAMS_4", "RAMS_5", "RAMS_6",
  "RAMS_7", "RAMS_8", "RAMS_9", "RAMS_10", "RAMS_11", "RAMS_12",
  "RAMS_13", "RAMS_14",

  "PAQ1_1", "PAQ1_2", "PAQ1_3", "PAQ1_4", "PAQ1_5", "PAQ1_6",
  "PAQ1_7", "PAQ1_8", "PAQ1_9", "PAQ1_10", "PAQ1_11", "PAQ1_12", 
  "PAQ1_13", "PAQ1_14",  "PAQ1_15",
  
  "PAQ2_1", "PAQ2_2", "PAQ2_3", "PAQ2_4", "PAQ2_5", "PAQ2_6", 
  "PAQ2_7", "PAQ2_8", "PAQ2_9", "PAQ2_10", "PAQ2_11", "PAQ2_12",
  "PAQ2_13", "PAQ2_14", "PAQ2_15"
)


rams_items_raw <- paste0("RAMS_", 1:14)
paq_items <- c(paste0("PAQ1_", 1:15), paste0("PAQ2_", 1:15))

study_data <- study_raw %>%
  select(all_of(study_vars)) %>%
  mutate(across(-condition, as.numeric)) %>%
  filter(if_all(all_of(paq_items), ~ !is.na(.))) %>%
  filter(if_all(all_of(rams_items_raw), ~ !is.na(.)))
```

### Check Analysis Dataset

```{r}
#| label: check_analysis_dataset
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

length(study_vars)

dim(study_data)

names(study_data)
study_data %>% count(condition)
```

The analysis dataset contains 76 participants across 58 variables. The
variable list confirms that all scale items are present, including 10
GSE items, 14 RAMS items, 30 PAQ items, and the experimental condition
variable. The dataset is ready for scale scoring and analysis.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Variable Preparation

This section prepares all variables needed for analysis. Scales scores
were computed for self-efficacy (GSE), achievement motivation (RAM), and
parenting warmth (PAQ) by summing each participant's item responses.
Perceived parental warmth was median-split into high and low groups to
create the moderator variable used in the ANOVA. A unique participant ID
was assigned to each row.

### Prepare Participant ID and Experimental Condition

```{r}
#| label: prepare_id_and_condition
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    id = row_number(),
    condition = factor(
      condition,
      levels = c(0, 1),
      labels = c("Control", "Autonomy")
    )
  )
```

A unique numeric ID was assigned to each participant and the
experimental condition variable was converted to a labeled factor with
two levels (Control and Autonomy).

### Check Experimental Condition Coding

```{r}
#| label: check_condition_coding
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data %>%
  count(condition) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Condition", "n")
  )
```

The "Control" condition contained 38 participants and the "Autonomy"
condition contained 38 participants, for a total of 76 participants
included in the analysis.

### Check Prepared Analysis Dataset

```{r}
#| label: check_prepared_analysis_dataset
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

dim(study_data)

names(study_data)
```

The final analysis dataset contains 76 participants across 59 variables,
confirming that all scale items, demographic variables, condition
assignment, and the participant ID are present and ready for analysis.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Reverse-Code Scale Items

Items requiring reverse coding were transformed before scale scores were
computed.

```{r}
#| label: reverse_code_items
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Reverse-code selected RAM items
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    RAMS_1r = 4 - RAMS_1, # reverse-codes RAMS_1
    RAMS_2r = 4 - RAMS_2, # reverse-codes RAMS_2
    RAMS_7r = 4 - RAMS_7, # reverse-codes RAMS_7
    RAMS_11r = 4 - RAMS_11, #reverse-codes RAMS_11
    RAMS_12r = 4 - RAMS_12, #reverse-codes RAMS_12
    RAMS_13r = 4 - RAMS_13, #reverse-codes RAMS_13
    RAMS_14r = 4 - RAMS_14, #reverse-codes RAMS_14
    )
# Compare original and reversed values
study_data %>%
  select(RAMS_2, RAMS_2r) %>% # selects variables for comparison
  head() # displays first rows
```

### Check Reverse Coding

```{r}
#| label: check_reverse_coding
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data %>%
  select(RAMS_1,RAMS_1r) %>%
  head(10) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

The reverse coding was applied correctly to the RAMS_1 item.

### Create Scale Scores

The scale scores were created by first defining the items belonging to
each scale. The GSE scale includes 10 self-efficacy items, the RAMS
scale includes 14 achievement motivation items with some reverse-coded
versions, and the PAQ items are grouped by parenting style
(Authoritarian, Authoritative, Permissive). Composite scores were then
computed by summing each participants responses across the relevant
items.

```{r}
#| label: create_scale_scores
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Define GSE scale items
GSE_items <- c("GSE_1","GSE_2","GSE_3","GSE_4", "GSE_5",
               "GSE_6","GSE_7","GSE_8","GSE_9","GSE_10"
               )

# Define RAM scale items
RAM_items <- c( "RAMS_1r","RAMS_2r","RAMS_3","RAMS_4","RAMS_5","RAMS_6","RAMS_7r","RAMS_8","RAMS_9","RAMS_10","RAMS_11r","RAMS_12r","RAMS_13r","RAMS_14r"
               )
# Check that all items exist in dataset
setNames(RAM_items %in% names(study_data), RAM_items) # verifies item presence

# Define permissive PAQ items 
PerPAQ_items <- c("PAQ1_1", "PAQ1_6", "PAQ1_10", "PAQ1_13", 
                  "PAQ1_14", "PAQ2_2", "PAQ2_4", "PAQ2_6", 
                  "PAQ2_9", "PAQ2_13"
                  )

# Define authoritarian PAQ items 
ATNPAQ_items <- c("PAQ1_2", "PAQ1_3", "PAQ1_7", "PAQ1_9", 
                  "PAQ1_12", "PAQ2_1", "PAQ2_3", "PAQ2_10", 
                  "PAQ2_11", "PAQ2_14"
                  )

# Define authoritative PAQ items 
ATEPAQ_items <-c("PAQ1_4", "PAQ1_5", "PAQ1_8", "PAQ1_11", 
                 "PAQ1_15", "PAQ2_5", "PAQ2_7", "PAQ2_8", 
                 "PAQ2_12", "PAQ2_15"
                 )

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    GSE_scale  = rowSums(across(all_of(GSE_items)),  na.rm = FALSE),
    RAM_scale  = rowSums(across(all_of(RAM_items)),  na.rm = FALSE),
    PerPAQ_scale  = rowSums(across(all_of(PerPAQ_items)),  na.rm = FALSE),
    ATNPAQ_scale  = rowSums(across(all_of(ATNPAQ_items)), na.rm = FALSE),
    ATEPAQ_scale  = rowSums(across(all_of(ATEPAQ_items)), na.rm = FALSE)
  )
```

### Check Scale Scores

```{r}
#| label: check_scale_scores
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data %>%
  select(GSE_scale, RAM_scale, PerPAQ_scale, ATNPAQ_scale, ATEPAQ_scale) %>%
  summary()

study_data %>%
  summarise(
    GSE_missing = mean(is.na(GSE_scale)),
    RAM_missing = mean(is.na(RAM_scale)),
    PerPAQ_missing = mean(is.na(PerPAQ_scale)),
    ATNPAQ_missing = mean(is.na(ATNPAQ_scale)),
    ATEPAQ_missing = mean(is.na(ATEPAQ_scale))
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

This scale scores check confirms that all five scale scores were
successfully computed with no missing values across any of the scales.

### Score Scale Distributions

#### GSE

```{r}
summary(study_data$GSE_scale) # should fall within expected range

median(study_data$GSE_scale, na.rm = TRUE)

```

#### RAMS

```{r}
summary(study_data$RAM_scale) # should fall within expected range

median(study_data$RAM_scale, na.rm = TRUE)

```

#### PerPAQ

```{r}
summary(study_data$PerPAQ_scale) # should fall within expected range

median(study_data$PerPAQ_scale, na.rm = TRUE)
```

#### ATNPAQ

```{r}
summary(study_data$ATNPAQ_scale) # should fall within expected range

median(study_data$ATNPAQ_scale, na.rm = TRUE)
```

#### ATEPAQ

```{r}
summary(study_data$ATEPAQ_scale) # should fall within expected range

median(study_data$ATEPAQ_scale, na.rm = TRUE)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Create Parental Warmth Groups

Perceived parental warmth groups were created using a median split of
scales. Participants scoring above the median were classified as "High
Warmth" and those at or bellow as "Low Warmth", creating the moderator
variable used in the ANOVA.

```{r}
#| label: create_parental_warmth_groups
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(pwarmth = case_when(
    ATNPAQ_scale > ATEPAQ_scale & ATNPAQ_scale > PerPAQ_scale ~"low", TRUE~"high"
  ))

study_data %>%
  count(pwarmth) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

The parental warmth grouping resulted in 48 participants reporting
having "High Warmth" and 28 reporting having "Low Warmth".

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Check Scale Reliability

Scale reliability was checked using Cronbach's alpha and McDonald's
omega for each measure. Values at or above .70 are considered
acceptable, while values above .80 indicate good reliability.

```{r}
#| label: check_scale_reliability
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

GSE_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(GSE_items))
RAM_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(RAM_items))
PerPAQ_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(PerPAQ_items))
ATNPAQ_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(ATNPAQ_items))
ATEPAQ_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(ATEPAQ_items))

GSE_alpha <- psych::alpha(GSE_item_df)
GSE_omega <- psych::omega(GSE_item_df, plot = FALSE)

RAM_alpha <- psych::alpha(RAM_item_df)
RAM_omega <- psych::omega(RAM_item_df, plot = FALSE)

PerPAQ_alpha <- psych::alpha(PerPAQ_item_df)
PerPAQ_omega <- psych::omega(PerPAQ_item_df, plot = FALSE)

ATNPAQ_alpha <- psych::alpha(ATNPAQ_item_df)
ATNPAQ_omega <- psych::omega(ATNPAQ_item_df, plot = FALSE)

ATEPAQ_alpha <- psych::alpha(ATEPAQ_item_df)
ATEPAQ_omega <- psych::omega(ATEPAQ_item_df, plot = FALSE)

reliability_table <- tibble(
  scale = c(
    "General Self-Efficacy",
    "Achievement Motivation",
    "Permissive Parenting",
    "Authoritarian Parenting",
    "Authoritative Parenting"
  ),
  alpha = c(
    GSE_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    RAM_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    PerPAQ_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    ATNPAQ_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    ATEPAQ_alpha$total$raw_alpha
  ),
  omega = c(
    GSE_omega$omega.tot,
    RAM_omega$omega.tot,
    PerPAQ_omega$omega.tot,
    ATNPAQ_omega$omega.tot,
    ATEPAQ_omega$omega.tot
  )
)

reliability_table %>%
  mutate(
    alpha = round(alpha, 2),
    omega = round(omega, 2)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Scale", "Cronbach's alpha", "McDonald's omega")
  )
```

The five scales demonstrated acceptable to excellent internal
consistency.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Descriptive Statistics

Descriptive statistics were computed for all five scale scores to
examine the distribution, central tendency, and variability of responses
prior to running inferential analyses.

### Scale Score Descriptives

```{r}
#| label: scale_score_descriptives
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data %>%
  select(
    GSE_scale,
    RAM_scale,
    PerPAQ_scale,
    ATNPAQ_scale,
    ATEPAQ_scale
  ) %>%
  psych::describe() %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
```

Across all 76 participants, self-efficacy scores averaged 31.16 (*SD* =
4.41), and achievement motivation scores averaged 24.64 (*SD* = 5.47).
For parenting style, permissive parenting averaged 25.92 (*SD* = 6.53),
authoritarian parenting averaged 30.91 (*SD* = 8.12), and authoritative
parenting averaged 33.13 (*SD* = 8.17). All scales distributions
appeared approximately normal based on the similarity between means and
medians.

### Sample Composition by Age, Gender, and Year

```{r}
#| label: sample_composition_categorical_variables
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# AGE
study_data %>%
  count(age) %>%
  arrange(age) %>%
  mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Age", "n", "Percent"),
    caption = "Sample Composition by Age"
  )

# GENDER
study_data %>%
  count(gender) %>%
  arrange(gender) %>%
  mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Gender", "n", "Percent"),
    caption = "Sample Composition by Gender"
  )

# YEAR
study_data %>%
  count(year) %>%
  arrange(year) %>%
  mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Year", "n", "Percent"),
    caption = "Sample Composition by Year"
  )
```

These results describe the sample composition based on year, age, and
gender.

### Correlation Tests (GSE & RAM)

```{r}
# Correlations for the general self-efficacy and achievement motivation scales
psych::corr.test(
  study_data %>% select(GSE_scale, RAM_scale)
)

# Scatterplots with labeled axes
ggplot(study_data, aes(x = GSE_scale, y = RAM_scale)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    x = "GSE Score", y = "RAM Score"
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Primary Analyses

This section includes the analyses of Academic Feedback Type and
Perceived Parental Warmth on Self-Efficacy.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### General Self-Efficacy Model

A 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA was conducted to examine the effects of the
Academic Feedback Condition and Perceived Parental Warmth on self
efficacy.

```{r}
#| label: anova_self_efficacy
#| echo: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA prediction Self-Efficacy scores
# DV: GSE_scale
# Factors: Condition and pwarmth

a1_SE <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "GSE_scale",
  between = c("condition", "pwarmth"),
  
data = study_data
)

a1_SE
```

This suggests there to be no significant main effect of feedback
condition on self-efficacy, *F*(1, 72) = 0.07, *p* = .791, *η²* \< .001,
and no significant main effect of parental warmth, *F*(1, 72) = 0.01,
*p* = .940, *η²* \< .001, and no significant interaction between
condition and parental warmth, *F*(1, 72) = 1.02, *p* = .315, *η²* =
.014.

### Estimated Means for Self-Efficacy

```{r}
#| label: estimated_means_self-efficacy
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

emmeans(a1_SE, ~ condition)

emmeans(a1_SE, ~ pwarmth)

emmeans(a1_SE, ~ condition * pwarmth)
```

Estimated marginal means indicated that self-efficacy scores were
similar across both feedback conditions.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Achievement Motivation Model

A 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA was conducted using the same factors to
examine the effects of the Academic Feedback Condition and Perceived
Parental Warmth on achievement motivation.

```{r}
#| label: anova_achievementmotivation
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

a1_RAM <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "RAM_scale",
  between = c("condition", "pwarmth"),
  data    = study_data %>%
              filter(!is.na(RAM_scale), !is.na(pwarmth), !is.na(condition)) %>%
              mutate(
                condition = factor(condition),
                pwarmth   = factor(pwarmth)
              )
)

a1_RAM
```

The ANOVA revealed no significant main effect of feedback condition on
achievement motivation, *F*(1, 72) = 0.02, *p* = .902, *η²* \< .001, no
significant main effect of parental warmth, *F*(1, 72) = 2.88, *p* =
.094, *η²* = 0.038, and no significant interaction between condition and
parental warmth, *F*(1, 72) = 0.00, *p* = .945, *η²* \< .001.

### Estimated Means for Achievement Motivation

```{r}
#| label: estimated_means_achievement motivation
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

emmeans(a1_RAM, ~ condition)

emmeans(a1_RAM, ~ pwarmth)

emmeans(a1_RAM, ~ condition * pwarmth)
```

Estimated marginal means indicated that achievement motivation scores
were similar across both feedback conditions, with the controlling
condition (*M* = 24.4, 95% CI \[22.6, 26.3\]) and autonomy-supportive
condition (*M* = 24.3, 95% CI \[22.4, 26.1\]) showing nearly identical
means. High warmth participants (*M* = 25.5, 95% CI \[23.9, 27.0\])
scored slightly higher than low warmth participants (*M* = 23.2, 95% CI
\[21.2, 25.3\]), though this difference was not statistically
significant. Cell means ranged from 23.2 to 25.6 across all four
conditions, consistent with the non-significant interaction.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Figures

The following figures display the estimated marginal means for each
dependent variable across the four conditions created by crossing
Academic Feedback Condition (Control vs. Autonomy) and Perceived
Parental Warmth (High vs. Low). The error bars represent 95% confidence
intervals.

### Self Efficacy Interaction Plot

```{r}
#| label: self_efficacy_interaction_plot
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Re-run ANOVA
a1_SE <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "GSE_scale",
  between = c("condition", "pwarmth"),
  data = study_data
)

# Create cell means
SE_cell_means <- as.data.frame(
  emmeans(a1_SE, ~ condition * pwarmth)
)

p_SE_bar <- ggplot(
  SE_cell_means,
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = pwarmth
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Feedback Condition",
    y = "Mean Self-Efficacy Score",
    fill = "Parental Warmth"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("lightblue", "salmon"))

p_SE_bar

```

**Figure 1**. This figure displays the cell means for self-efficacy
across feedback condition and perceived parental warmth. Error bars
represent 95% confidence intervals.

### Achievement Motivation Interaction Plot

```{r}
#| label: achievement_motivation_interaction_plot
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

p_RAM_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a1_RAM, ~ condition * pwarmth)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = pwarmth
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Feedback Condition",
    y = "Mean Achievement Motivation",
    fill = "Parental Warmth"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("lightblue", "salmon"))

p_RAM_bar
```

**Figure 2**. This figure displays the cell means for achievement
motivation across feedback condition and perceived parental warmth.
Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

### Export Figures

```{r}
#| label: save_figures
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

if (!dir.exists("figures")) {
  dir.create("figures")
}

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/self_efficacy_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_SE_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/achievement_motivation_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_RAM_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)
```

Both interaction plots were exported as svg files to the figures folder
for use in the final poster and report. Figure 1 displays the
self-efficacy interaction plot and Figure 2 displays the achievement
motivation interaction plot.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Results Summary

Overall, the results of this study did not support our hypotheses.
Participants who received autonomy-supportive academic feedback did not
report higher self-efficacy or achievement motivation compared to those
who received controlling feedback. Similarly, perceived parental warmth
did not significantly influence either outcome. The interaction between
feedback condition and parental warmth was also non-significant for both
self-efficacy and achievement motivation, suggesting that parental
warmth did not change how students responded to different types of
academic feedback. These null findings may reflect limitations in sample
size, the use of a hypothetical feedback scenario, or the possibility
that a single exposure to feedback is not enough to meaningfully shift
self-efficacy or motivation.

## Reproducibility Information

This section documents the R version, operating system, and package
versions used to run and render this report. Recording this information
makes it possible for others to replicate our analyses using the same
software environment.

```{r}
#| label: session_info
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

sessionInfo()
```
